question_text: If 10 9 electrons move out of a body to another body every second, how much time is required to get a total charge of 1 C on the other body?
question_html: If 109 electrons move out of a body to another body every second, how much time is required to get a total charge of 1 C on the other body?
solution_text: In one second, 10 9 electrons move out of the body. Therefore the charge given out in one second is 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 C = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 C. Thus 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 C of charge moves out of the body to another body every second. The time required to accumulate a charge of 1C can then be estimated to be: 1 C {tex}\div{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 C/s) = 6.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 s Now convert it into years, 6.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 {tex}\div{/tex} (365 {tex}\times{/tex} 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 3600) years = 198 years. Thus to collect a charge of one coulomb, from a body from which 10 9 electrons move out every second, we will need approximately 200 years. One coulomb is, therefore, a very large unit for many practical purposes. It is, however, also important to know what is roughly the number of electrons contained in a piece of one cubic centimetre of material. A cubic piece of copper of side 1 cm contains about 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 24 electrons.
solution_html:
In one second, 109 electrons move out of the body. Therefore the charge given out in one second is
1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 C = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 C.
Thus 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 C of charge moves out of the body to another body every second. The time required to accumulate a charge of 1C can then be estimated to be:
1 C {tex}\div{/tex}1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 C/s) = 6.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 s
Now convert it into years,
6.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 {tex}\div{/tex} (365 {tex}\times{/tex} 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 3600) years = 198 years.
Thus to collect a charge of one coulomb, from a body from which 109 electrons move out every second, we will need approximately 200 years. One coulomb is, therefore, a very large unit for many practical purposes.
It is, however, also important to know what is roughly the number of electrons contained in a piece of one cubic centimetre of material. A cubic piece of copper of side 1 cm contains about 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 electrons.

question_text: How can you charge a metal sphere positively without touching it?
question_html: How can you charge a metal sphere positively without touching it?
solution_text: By Induction Method, a charged body A imparts to another body B, some charge of the opposite sign without any actual contact between A and B. Also, body A shall not lose any charge as it is not in contact with B. To illustrate it let us consider, figure (a) shows an uncharged metallic sphere on an insulating metal stand. Bring a negatively charged rod close to the metallic sphere, as shown in Fig (b). As the rod is brought close to the sphere, the free electrons in the sphere move away due to repulsion and start piling up at the farther end. The near-end becomes positively charged due to a deficit of electrons. This process of charge distribution stops when the net force on the free electrons inside the metal is zero. Connect the sphere to the ground by a conducting wire. The electrons will flow to the ground while the positive charges at the near end will remain held there due to the attractive force of the negative charges on the rod, as shown in Fig (c). Disconnect the sphere from the ground. The positive charge continues to be held at the near end [Fig (d)]. Remove the electrified rod. The positive charge will spread uniformly over the sphere as shown in Fig (e). In this experiment, the metal sphere gets charged by the process of induction and the rod does not lose any of its charges.
solution_html:
By Induction Method, a charged body A imparts to another body B, some charge of the opposite sign without any actual contact between A and B. Also, body A shall not lose any charge as it is not in contact with B.
To illustrate it let us consider, figure (a) shows an uncharged metallic sphere on an insulating metal stand. Bring a negatively charged rod close to the metallic sphere, as shown in Fig (b). As the rod is brought close to the sphere, the free electrons in the sphere move away due to repulsion and start piling up at the farther end. The near-end becomes positively charged due to a deficit of electrons. This process of charge distribution stops when the net force on the free electrons inside the metal is zero. Connect the sphere to the ground by a conducting wire. The electrons will flow to the ground while the positive charges at the near end will remain held there due to the attractive force of the negative charges on the rod, as shown in Fig (c). Disconnect the sphere from the ground. The positive charge continues to be held at the near end [Fig (d)]. Remove the electrified rod. The positive charge will spread uniformly over the sphere as shown in Fig (e).

In this experiment, the metal sphere gets charged by the process of induction and the rod does not lose any of its charges.

question_text: How much positive and negative charge is there in a cup of water?
question_html: How much positive and negative charge is there in a cup of water?
solution_text: Let us assume that the mass of one cup of water is 250 g. The molecular mass of water is, 2 + 16 = 18g Thus, number of molecules in 18 g of water is = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 23 Therefore the number of molecules in one cup of water is ( {tex}\frac{250}{18}{/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 23 . Each molecule of water contains two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom, i.e., 10 electrons and 10 protons. Hence the total positive and total negative charge has the same magnitude. It is equal to ( {tex}\frac{250}{18}{/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 23 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C = 1.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 C
solution_html:
Let us assume that the mass of one cup of water is 250 g.
The molecular mass of water is, 2 + 16 = 18g
Thus, number of molecules in 18 g of water is = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
Therefore the number of molecules in one cup of water is ({tex}\frac{250}{18}{/tex}) {tex}\times{/tex} 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023.
Each molecule of water contains two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom, i.e., 10 electrons and 10 protons. Hence the total positive and total negative charge has the same magnitude.
It is equal to ({tex}\frac{250}{18}{/tex}) {tex}\times{/tex} 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C = 1.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 C

question_text: Coulomb’s law for the electrostatic force between two point charges and Newton’s law for gravitational force between two stationary point masses, both have inverse-square dependence on the distance between the charges and masses respectively. Compare the strength of these forces by determining the ratio of their magnitudes for an electron and a proton and for two protons. Estimate the accelerations of electron and proton due to the electrical force of their mutual attraction when they are 1 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}}{/tex} (= 10 -10 m) apart? (m p = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg, m e = 9.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg).
question_html:
Coulomb’s law for the electrostatic force between two point charges and Newton’s law for gravitational force between two stationary point masses, both have inverse-square dependence on the distance between the charges and masses respectively.

  1. Compare the strength of these forces by determining the ratio of their magnitudes
    1. for an electron and a proton and
    2. for two protons.
  2. Estimate the accelerations of electron and proton due to the electrical force of their mutual attraction when they are 1{tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}}{/tex} (= 10-10 m) apart? (mp = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg, me = 9.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg).
solution_text: we know that electric force is stronger than of the gravitational field, which can be proved as: The electric force between an electron and a proton at a distance r apart is(magnitude of electric force between two point-like charges is given by the Coulomb force) F e = {tex}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} where the negative sign indicates that the force is attractive. The corresponding gravitational force (always attractive) is: F G = {tex}-G \frac{m_{p} m_{e}}{r^{2}}{/tex} where m p and m e are the masses of a proton and an electron respectively. {tex}\left|\frac{F_{e}}{F_{G}}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} G m_{p} m_{e}}{/tex} = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 39 On similar lines, the ratio of the magnitudes of electric force to the gravitational force between two protons at a distance r apart is: {tex}\left|\frac{F_{e}}{F_{G}}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} G m_{p} m_{p}}{/tex} = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 36 However, it may be mentioned here that the signs of the two forces are different. For two protons, the gravitational force is attractive in nature and the Coulomb force is repulsive (in like charges). The actual values of these forces between two protons inside a nucleus (distance between two protons is {tex}\sim{/tex} 10 -15 m inside a nucleus) are F e {tex}\sim{/tex} 230 N, whereas, F G {tex}\sim{/tex} 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 N, faces the repulsive force due to like charges. The (dimensionless) ratio of the two forces shows that electrical forces are enormously stronger than the gravitational forces. The electric force F exerted by a proton on an electron is the same in magnitude to the force exerted by an electron on a proton; however, the masses of an electron and a proton are different. Thus, the magnitude of the force is |F| = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} = 8.987 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 Nm 2 /C 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C) 2 /(10 -10 m) 2 = 2.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 N Using Newton’s second law of motion, F = ma, the acceleration that an electron will undergo is a = 2.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 N / 9.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 22 m/s 2 ​​​​​​​Comparing this with the value of acceleration due to gravity, we can conclude that the effect of the gravitational field is negligible on the motion of the electron and it undergoes very large accelerations under the action of Coulomb force due to a proton. ​​​​​​​The value for acceleration of the proton is 2.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 N / 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg = 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 19 m/s 2
solution_html:
1.  we know that electric force is stronger than of the gravitational field, which can be proved as:
      The electric force between an electron and a proton at a distance r apart is(magnitude of electric force between two point-like charges is given by the Coulomb force)
      Fe = {tex}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} where the negative sign indicates that the force is attractive. The corresponding gravitational force (always attractive) is:
      FG = {tex}-G \frac{m_{p} m_{e}}{r^{2}}{/tex} 
      where mp and me are the masses of a proton and an electron respectively. 
      {tex}\left|\frac{F_{e}}{F_{G}}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} G m_{p} m_{e}}{/tex} = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1039 
      On similar lines, the ratio of the magnitudes of electric force to the gravitational force between two protons at a distance r apart is: 
      {tex}\left|\frac{F_{e}}{F_{G}}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} G m_{p} m_{p}}{/tex} = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1036 
    2. However, it may be mentioned here that the signs of the two forces are different. For two protons, the gravitational force is attractive in nature and the Coulomb force is repulsive (in like charges). The actual values of these forces between two protons inside a nucleus (distance between two protons is {tex}\sim{/tex} 10-15 m inside a nucleus) are Fe {tex}\sim{/tex} 230 N, whereas, FG {tex}\sim{/tex} 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 N, faces the repulsive force due to like charges.   2. The electric force F exerted by a proton on an electron is the same in magnitude to the force exerted by an electron on a proton; however, the masses of an electron and a proton are different. Thus, the magnitude of the force is
    |F| = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} = 8.987 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 Nm2/C2 {tex}\times{/tex} (1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19C)2 /(10-10 m)2 = 2.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 N

    ​​​​​​​Comparing this with the value of acceleration due to gravity, we can conclude that the effect of the gravitational field is negligible on the motion of the electron and it undergoes very large accelerations under the action of Coulomb force due to a proton.
    ​​​​​​​The value for acceleration of the proton is 2.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 N / 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg = 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1019 m/s2

question_text: A charged metallic sphere A is suspended by a nylon thread. Another charged metallic sphere B held by an insulating handle is brought close to A such that the distance between their centres is 10 cm, as shown in Fig (a). The resulting repulsion of A is noted (for example, by shining a beam of light and measuring the deflection of its shadow on a screen). Spheres A and B are touched by uncharged spheres C and D respectively, as shown in Fig (b). C and D are then removed and B is brought closer to A distance of 5.0 cm between their centres, as shown in Fig (c). What is the expected repulsion of A on the basis of Coulomb’s law? Spheres A and C and spheres B and D have identical sizes. Ignore the sizes of A and B in comparison to the separation between their centres.
question_html: A charged metallic sphere A is suspended by a nylon thread. Another charged metallic sphere B held by an insulating handle is brought close to A such that the distance between their centres is 10 cm, as shown in Fig (a). The resulting repulsion of A is noted (for example, by shining a beam of light and measuring the deflection of its shadow on a screen). Spheres A and B are touched by uncharged spheres C and D respectively, as shown in Fig (b). C and D are then removed and B is brought closer to A distance of 5.0 cm between their centres, as shown in Fig (c). What is the expected repulsion of A on the basis of Coulomb’s law? Spheres A and C and spheres B and D have identical sizes. Ignore the sizes of A and B in comparison to the separation between their centres.
solution_text: Let the original charge on sphere A be q and that on B be q′. At a distance r between their centers, the magnitude of the electrostatic force on each is given by: F = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q q^{\prime}}{r^{2}}{/tex} Neglecting the sizes of spheres A and B in comparison to r. When an identical but uncharged sphere C touches A, the charges redistribute on A and C, and by symmetry, each sphere carries a charge {tex}\frac{q}{2}{/tex} ,(due to induction). Similarly, after D touches B, the redistributed charge on each is {tex}\frac{q'}{2}{/tex} . Now, if the separation between A and B is halved, the magnitude of the electrostatic force on each is F' = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(q / 2)\left(q^{\prime} / 2\right)}{(r / 2)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\left(q q^{\prime}\right)}{r^{2}}{/tex} = F As force is repulsive in nature .Thus the electrostatic force on A, due to B, remains unaltered.
solution_html:
Let the original charge on sphere A be q and that on B be q′. At a distance r between their centers, the magnitude of the electrostatic force on each is given by:
F = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q q^{\prime}}{r^{2}}{/tex} 
Neglecting the sizes of spheres A and B in comparison to r. When an identical but uncharged sphere C touches A, the charges redistribute on A and C, and by symmetry, each sphere carries a charge {tex}\frac{q}{2}{/tex},(due to induction).
Similarly, after D touches B, the redistributed charge on each is {tex}\frac{q'}{2}{/tex}. Now, if the separation between A and B is halved, the magnitude of the electrostatic force on each is
F' = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(q / 2)\left(q^{\prime} / 2\right)}{(r / 2)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\left(q q^{\prime}\right)}{r^{2}}{/tex} = F 
As force is repulsive in nature .Thus the electrostatic force on A, due to B, remains unaltered.

question_text: Consider three charges q 1 , q 2 , q 3 each equal to q at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side l. What is the force on a charge Q (with the same sign as q) placed at the centroid of the triangle, as shown in a fig?
question_html: Consider three charges q1, q2, q3 each equal to q at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side l. What is the force on a charge Q (with the same sign as q) placed at the centroid of the triangle, as shown in a fig?
solution_text: In the given equilateral triangle ABC of sides of length l, if we draw a perpendicular AD to the side BC, AD = AC cos 30º = ( {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} )l and the distance AO of the centroid O from A is ( {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} ) AD = ( {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} )l. By symmetry AO = BO = CO. Thus, Force F 1 on Q due to charge q at A = {tex}\frac{3}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Q q}{l^{2}}{/tex} along with AO. Force F 2 on Q due to charge q at B = {tex}\frac{3}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Qq}{l^{2}}{/tex} along with BO. Force F 3 on Q due to charge q at C = {tex}\frac{3}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Qq}{l^{2}}{/tex} along with CO, All the forces are the same in magnitude. The resultant of forces F 2 and F 3 is {tex}\frac{3}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Qq}{l^{2}}{/tex} along OA, by the parallelogram law. Therefore, the total force on Q = {tex}\frac{3}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Q q}{l^{2}}(\hat{\mathbf{r}}-\hat{\mathbf{r}}){/tex} = 0, where {tex}\hat r{/tex} is the unit vector along OA. It is clear also by symmetry that the three forces will sum to zero. Suppose that the resultant force was non-zero but in some direction.
solution_html:
In the given equilateral triangle ABC of sides of length l, if we draw a perpendicular AD to the side BC, AD = AC cos 30º = ({tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex})l and the distance AO of the centroid O from A is ({tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}) AD = ({tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex})l. By symmetry AO = BO = CO.
Thus,
Force F1 on Q due to charge q at A = {tex}\frac{3}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Q q}{l^{2}}{/tex} along with AO.
Force F2 on Q due to charge q at B = {tex}\frac{3}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Qq}{l^{2}}{/tex} along with BO.
Force F3 on Q due to charge q at C = {tex}\frac{3}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Qq}{l^{2}}{/tex} along with CO,  All the forces are the same in magnitude.
The resultant of forces F2 and F3 is {tex}\frac{3}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Qq}{l^{2}}{/tex} along OA, by the parallelogram law. Therefore, the total force on Q = {tex}\frac{3}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Q q}{l^{2}}(\hat{\mathbf{r}}-\hat{\mathbf{r}}){/tex} = 0, where {tex}\hat r{/tex} is the unit vector along OA.
It is clear also by symmetry that the three forces will sum to zero. Suppose that the resultant force was non-zero but in some direction.

question_text: Consider the charges q, q, and –q placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle, as shown in fig. What is the force on each charge?
question_html: Consider the charges q, q, and –q placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle, as shown in fig. What is the force on each charge?
solution_text: The forces acting on charge q at A due to charges q at B and –q at C are F 12 along with BA and F 13 along with AC respectively, as shown in fig. By the parallelogram law, the total force F 1 on the charge q at A is given by F 1 = {tex}F \hat r_1{/tex} where {tex} \hat r_1{/tex} is a unit vector along with BC. The force of attraction or repulsion for each pair of charges has the same magnitude F = {tex}\frac{q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} l^{2}}{/tex} The total force F 2 on charge q at B is thus F 2 = {tex}F \hat r_2{/tex} , where {tex} \hat r_2{/tex} is a unit vector along with AC. Similarly, the total force on a charge –q at C is F 3 = {tex}\sqrt{3} F \hat n{/tex} , where {tex}\hat n{/tex} is the unit vector along the direction bisecting the {tex}\angle{/tex} BCA. or F 1 is calculated by = {tex}\sqrt{(F_2^2+F_3^{2}+(2\times F_2 \times F_3 ) cos60)}{/tex} and F 2 and F 3a are equal in magnitude. So resultant F 1 = {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} F. It is interesting to see that the sum of the forces on the three charges is zero, i.e., F 1 + F 2 + F 3 = 0 The result is not at all surprising. It follows straight from the fact that Coulomb’s law is consistent with Newton’s third law.

The forces acting on charge q at A due to charges q at B and –q at C are F12 along with BA and F13 along with AC respectively, as shown in fig. By the parallelogram law, the total force F1 on the charge q at A is given by
F1 = {tex}F \hat r_1{/tex} where {tex} \hat r_1{/tex} is a unit vector along with BC.
The force of attraction or repulsion for each pair of charges has the same magnitude F = {tex}\frac{q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} l^{2}}{/tex} 
The total force F2 on charge q at B is thus F2 = {tex}F \hat r_2{/tex}, where {tex} \hat r_2{/tex} is a unit vector along with AC.
Similarly, the total force on a charge –q at C is F3 = {tex}\sqrt{3} F \hat n{/tex}, where {tex}\hat n{/tex} is the unit vector along the direction bisecting the {tex}\angle{/tex}BCA. 
or F1 is calculated by ={tex}\sqrt{(F_2^2+F_3^{2}+(2\times F_2 \times F_3 ) cos60)}{/tex} and F2 and F3a are equal in magnitude. So resultant F1 = {tex}\sqrt3{/tex}F. It is interesting to see that the sum of the forces on the three charges is zero, i.e.,
F1 + F2 + F3 = 0
The result is not at all surprising. It follows straight from the fact that Coulomb’s law is consistent with Newton’s third law.

question_text: An electron falls through a distance of 1.5 cm in a uniform electric field of magnitude 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 N C -1 [Fig (a)]. The direction of the field is reversed keeping its magnitude unchanged and a proton falls through the same distance [Fig (b)]. Compute the time of fall in each case. Contrast the situation with that of free fall under gravity.
question_html: An electron falls through a distance of 1.5 cm in a uniform electric field of magnitude 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 N C-1 [Fig (a)]. The direction of the field is reversed keeping its magnitude unchanged and a proton falls through the same distance [Fig (b)]. Compute the time of fall in each case. Contrast the situation with that of free fall under gravity.
solution_text: In figure (a): The field is upward, so the negatively charged particle, electron experiences a downward force of magnitude eE where E is the magnitude of the electric field. {tex}a=\frac{e \mathrm{E}}{m_e}=\frac{1 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2.0 \times 10^{4}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}=3.516 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{ms}^{-2}{/tex} Now, u = 0 and S = 1.5 cm = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m Using the relation: S = ut + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} at 2 , we get t = 2.92 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 s In figure (b): The field is downward, and the positively charged proton experiences a downward force of magnitude eE . The acceleration of the proton is:- {tex}a=\frac{e \mathrm{E}}{m_p}=\frac{1 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \cdot 0 \times 10^{4}}{1 \cdot 67 \times 10^{-27}}{/tex} = 1.916 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 12 ms -2 Now, u = 0 and S = 1.5 cm = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m Using the relation: S = ut + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} at 2 , we get t = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 s

Now, u = 0 and S = 1.5 cm = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 m

question_text: Two-point charges q 1 and q 2 , of magnitude +10 -8 C and -10 -8 C, respectively, are placed 0.1 m apart. Calculate the electric fields at points A, B, and C shown in fig.
question_html: Two-point charges q1 and q2, of magnitude +10-8 C and -10-8 C, respectively, are placed 0.1 m apart. Calculate the electric fields at points A, B, and C shown in fig.
solution_text: The electric field due to a charge is given by the formula E= {tex}q/4\pi\in0(r^2){/tex} . Thus, the electric field vector E 1A at A due to the positive charge q 1 points towards the right and has a magnitude E 1A = {tex}\frac{\left(9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} \mathrm{C}^{-2}\right) \times\left(10^{-8} \mathrm{C}\right)}{(0.05 \mathrm{m})^{2}}{/tex} = 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 NC -1 The electric field vector E 2A at A due to the negative charge q 2 points towards the right and has the same magnitude. Hence the magnitude of the total electric field E A at A is E A = E 1A + E 2A = 7.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 NC -1 E A is directed toward the right. The electric field vector E 1B at B due to the positive charge q 1 points towards the left and has a magnitude E 1B = {tex}\frac{\left(9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} \mathrm{C}^{-2}\right) \times\left(10^{-8} \mathrm{C}\right)}{(0.05 \mathrm{m})^{2}}{/tex} = 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 NC -1 The electric field vector E 2B at B due to the negative charge q 2 points towards the right and has a magnitude E 2B = {tex}\frac{\left(9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} \mathrm{C}^{-2}\right) \times\left(10^{-8} \mathrm{C}\right)}{(0.15 \mathrm{m})^{2}}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 NC -1 The magnitude of the total electric field at B is E B = E 1B – E 2B = 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 NC -1 E B is directed towards the left. The magnitude of each electric field vector at point C, due to charge q 1 and q 2 is E 1C = E 2C = {tex}\frac{\left(9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} \mathrm{C}^{-2}\right) \times\left(10^{-8} \mathrm{C}\right)}{(0.10 \mathrm{m})^{2}}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 NC -1 The directions in which these two vectors point are indicated in fig. The resultant of these two vectors is E C = {tex}E_{1 C} \cos \frac{\pi}{3}+E_{2 C} \cos \frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 NC -1 E C points towards the right.

The electric field due to a charge is given by the formula E={tex}q/4\pi\in0(r^2){/tex}. Thus, the electric field vector E1A at A due to the positive charge q1 points towards the right and has a magnitude
E1A = {tex}\frac{\left(9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} \mathrm{C}^{-2}\right) \times\left(10^{-8} \mathrm{C}\right)}{(0.05 \mathrm{m})^{2}}{/tex} = 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 NC-1

EC points towards the right.

question_text: Two charges {tex}\pm{/tex} 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} C are placed 5.0 mm apart. Determine the electric field at a point on the axis of the dipole 15 cm away from its centre O on the side of the positive charge, as shown in figure. a point Q, 15 cm away from O on a line passing through O and normal to the axis of the dipolem, as shown in figure.
question_html:
Two charges {tex}\pm{/tex}10 {tex}\mu{/tex}C are placed 5.0 mm apart. Determine the electric field at

  1. a point on the axis of the dipole 15 cm away from its centre O on the side of the positive charge, as shown in figure.
  2. a point Q, 15 cm away from O on a line passing through O and normal to the axis of the dipolem, as shown in figure.
solution_text: Field at P due to charge +10 {tex}\mu{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{10^{-5} \mathrm{C}}{4 \pi\left(8.854 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} \mathrm{N}^{-1} \mathrm{m}^{-2}\right)} \times \frac{1}{(15-0.25)^{2} \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{m}^{2}}{/tex} = 4.13 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 NC -1 along BP Field at P due to charge -10 {tex}\mu{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{10^{-5} \mathrm{C}}{4 \pi\left(8.854 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} \mathrm{N}^{-1} \mathrm{m}^{-2}\right)} \times \frac{1}{(15+0.25)^{2} \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{m}^{2}}{/tex} = 3.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 NC -1 along PA The resultant electric field at P due to the two charges at A and B is = 2.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 NC -1 along BP. In this example, the ratio OP/OB is quite large (= 60). Thus, we can expect to get approximately the same result as above by directly using the formula for electric field at a far-away point on the axis of a dipole. For a dipole consisting of charges {tex}\pm{/tex} q, 2a distance apart, the electric field at a distance r from the centre on the axis of the dipole has a magnitude E = {tex}\frac{2 p}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3}}{/tex} (r/a >> 1) where p = 2aq is the magnitude of the dipole moment. The direction of electric field on the dipole axis is always along the direction of the dipole moment vector (i.e., from -q to q). Here, p = 10 -5 C {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 Cm Therefore, electric field is given by: E = {tex}\frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{Cm}}{4 \pi\left(8.854 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} \mathrm{N}^{-1} \mathrm{m}^{-2}\right)} \times \frac{1}{(15)^{3} \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{m}^{3}}{/tex} = 2.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 NC -1 along the dipole moment direction AB, which is close to the result obtained earlier. Field at Q due to charge + 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} C at B = {tex}\frac{10^{-5} \mathrm{C}}{4 \pi\left(8.854 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} \mathrm{N}^{-1} \mathrm{m}^{-2}\right)} \times \frac{1}{\left[15^{2}+(0.25)^{2}\right] \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{m}^{2}}{/tex} = 3.99 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 NC -1 along BQ Field at Q due to charge –10 {tex}\mu{/tex} C at A = {tex}\frac{10^{-5} \mathrm{C}}{4 \pi\left(8.854 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} \mathrm{N}^{-1} \mathrm{m}^{-2}\right)} \times \frac{1}{\left[15^{2}+(0.25)^{2}\right] \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{m}^{2}}{/tex} = 3.99 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 6 NC -1 along QA Clearly, the components of these two forces with equal magnitudes cancel along the direction OQ but add up along the direction parallel to BA. Therefore, the resultant electric field at Q due to the two charges at A and B is = {tex}2 \times \frac{0.25}{\sqrt{15^{2}+(0.25)^{2}}} \times 3.99 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{N} \mathrm{C}^{-1}{/tex} along BA = 1.33 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 NC -1 along BA. As in (a), we can expect to get approximately the same result by directly using the formula for dipole field at a point on the normal to the axis of the dipole: Electric field is given by ,E = {tex}\frac{p}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3}}{/tex} [r/a >> 1) = {tex}\frac{5 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{Cm}}{4 \pi\left(8.854 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} \mathrm{N}^{-1} \mathrm{m}^{-2}\right)} \times \frac{1}{(15)^{3} \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{m}^{3}}{/tex} E = 1.33 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 NC -1 The direction of the electric field in this case, is opposite to the direction of the dipole moment vector. Again, the result agrees with that obtained before.

question_html:
The electric field components in fig. are Ex = {tex}\alpha{/tex}x1/2, Ey = Ez = 0, in which {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 800 N/C m1/2. Calculate

  1. the flux through the cube, and
  2. the charge within the cube. Assume that a = 0.1 m.
solution_text: Since the electric field has only an x component, for faces perpendicular to the x direction, the angle between E and {tex}\Delta{/tex} S is {tex}\pm \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . Therefore, the flux {tex}\phi{/tex} = E. {tex}\Delta{/tex} S is separately zero for each face of the cube except the two shaded ones (because of the same magnitude). Now the magnitude of the electric field at the left face is E L = {tex}\alpha{/tex} x 1/2 = {tex}\alpha{/tex} a 1/2 (x = a at the left face). The magnitude of electric field at the right face is E R = {tex}\alpha{/tex} x 1/2 = {tex}\alpha{/tex} (2a) 1/2 (x = 2a at the right face). The corresponding fluxes are {tex}\phi_{L}={E}_{L} \cdot \Delta {S}=\Delta S {E}_{L} \cdot \hat{{n}}_{L}{/tex} = {tex}E_{L} \Delta S \cos \theta{/tex} = {tex}-E_{L} \Delta S,{/tex} Since {tex}\theta{/tex} = 180 o = -E L a 2 {tex}\phi_{R}={E}_{R} \cdot \Delta {S}=E_{R} \Delta S \cos \theta=E_{R} \Delta S{/tex} , since {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o = E R a 2 Net flux through the cube {tex}\phi_{R}+\phi_{L}=E_{R} a^{2}-E_{L} a^{2}=a^{2}\left(E_{R}-E_{L}\right){/tex} = {tex}\alpha a^{2}\left[(2 a)^{1 / 2}-a^{1 / 2}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\alpha a^{5 / 2}(\sqrt{2}-1){/tex} = {tex}800(0.1)^{5 / 2}(\sqrt{2}-1){/tex} = 1.05 Nm 2 C -1 We can use Gauss’s law to find the total charge q inside the cube. We have {tex}\phi = \frac{q}{\in_o}{/tex} or {tex}q = \phi \in_o{/tex} . Therefore, q = 1.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.854 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 C = 9.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 C it gives the total charge enclosed by the surface.

question_text: An early model for an atom considered it to have a positively charged point nucleus of charge Ze, surrounded by a uniform density of negative charge up to a radius R. The atom as a whole is neutral. For this model, what is the electric field at a distance r from the nucleus?
question_html: An early model for an atom considered it to have a positively charged point nucleus of charge Ze, surrounded by a uniform density of negative charge up to a radius R. The atom as a whole is neutral. For this model, what is the electric field at a distance r from the nucleus?
solution_text: The charge distribution for this model of the atom is as shown in Fig. The total negative charge in the uniform spherical charge distribution of radius R must be Ze, since the atom (nucleus of charge Ze+ negative charge) is neutral. This immediately gives us the negative charge density ρ, since we must have {tex}\frac{4 \pi R^{3}}{3} \rho{/tex} = 0 - Ze or {tex}\rho=-\frac{3 Z e}{4 \pi R^{3}}{/tex} To find the electric field E(r) at a point P which is a distance r away from the nucleus, we use Gauss’s law. Because of the spherical symmetry of the charge distribution, the magnitude of the electric field E(r) depends only on the radial distance, no matter what the direction of r. Its direction is along (or opposite to) the radius vector r from the origin to the point P. The obvious Gaussian surface is a spherical surface centered at the nucleus. We consider two situations, namely, r < R and r > R. r < R: The electric flux {tex}\phi{/tex} enclosed by the spherical surface is {tex}\phi{/tex} = E(r) {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 here area is (due to spherical) where E (r) is the magnitude of the electric field at r. This is because the field at any point on the spherical Gaussian surface has the same direction as the normal to the surface there, and has the same magnitude at all points on the surface. Firstly we find the total charge enclosed which is:- The charge q enclosed by the Gaussian surface is the positive nuclear charge and the negative charge within the sphere of radius r,(in simple terms charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface) i.e., q = Ze + {tex}\frac{4 \pi r^{3}}{3} \rho{/tex} Substituting for the charge density {tex}\rho{/tex} obtained earlier, we have q = Ze - Ze {tex}\frac{r^{3}}{R^{3}}{/tex} Gauss’s law then gives, E(r) = {tex}\frac{Z e}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{1}{r^{2}}-\frac{r}{R^{3}}{/tex} ; r < R The electric field is directed radially outward. r > R: In this case, the total charge enclosed by the Gaussian spherical surface is zero since the atom is neutral. (positive and negative charge balance each other)Thus, from Gauss’s law, E(r) {tex}\pi{/tex} 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = 0 or E (r) = 0; r > R At r = R, both cases give the same result: E = 0.
solution_html:
The charge distribution for this model of the atom is as shown in Fig. The total negative charge in the uniform spherical charge distribution of radius R must be Ze, since the atom (nucleus of charge Ze+ negative charge) is neutral. This immediately gives us the negative charge density ρ, since we must have
{tex}\frac{4 \pi R^{3}}{3} \rho{/tex} = 0 - Ze
or {tex}\rho=-\frac{3 Z e}{4 \pi R^{3}}{/tex} 
To find the electric field E(r) at a point P which is a distance r away from the nucleus, we use Gauss’s law. Because of the spherical symmetry of the charge distribution, the magnitude of the electric field E(r) depends only on the radial distance, no matter what the direction of r. Its direction is along (or opposite to) the radius vector r from the origin to the point P. The obvious Gaussian surface is a spherical surface centered at the nucleus. We consider two situations, namely, r < R and r > R.

  1. r < R: The electric flux {tex}\phi{/tex} enclosed by the spherical surface is
    {tex}\phi{/tex} = E(r) {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r2  here area is (due to spherical)
    where E (r) is the magnitude of the electric field at r. This is because the field at any point on the spherical Gaussian surface has the same direction as the normal to the surface there, and has the same magnitude at all points on the surface. Firstly we find the total charge enclosed which is:-
    The charge q enclosed by the Gaussian surface is the positive nuclear charge and the negative charge within the sphere of radius r,(in simple terms charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface)
    i.e., q = Ze + {tex}\frac{4 \pi r^{3}}{3} \rho{/tex} 
    Substituting for the charge density {tex}\rho{/tex} obtained earlier, we have
    q = Ze - Ze {tex}\frac{r^{3}}{R^{3}}{/tex} 
    Gauss’s law then gives,
    E(r) = {tex}\frac{Z e}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{1}{r^{2}}-\frac{r}{R^{3}}{/tex}; r < R

    At r = R, both cases give the same result: E = 0.

subject: Physics
question_text: Explain the meaning of the statement, electric charge of a body is quantized. Why can one ignore quantization of electric charge when dealing with macroscopic i.e. large scale charges?
question_html:
1. Explain the meaning of the statement, electric charge of a body is quantized.
  2. Why can one ignore quantization of electric charge when dealing with macroscopic i.e. large scale charges?
solution_text: Electric charge of a body is quantized. This means that only integral (1, 2… n) number of electrons can be transferred from one body to the other. Charges are not transferred in fraction. Hence, a body possesses total charge only in integral multiples of electric charge. In macroscopic or large scale charges, the charges used are huge as compared to the magnitude of electric charge. Hence, quantization of electric charge is of no use on macroscopic scale. Therefore, it is ignored and it is considered that electric charge is continuous.
solution_html:
1. Electric charge of a body is quantized. This means that only integral (1, 2… n) number of electrons can be transferred from one body to the other. Charges are not transferred in fraction. Hence, a body possesses total charge only in integral multiples of electric charge.
  2. In macroscopic or large scale charges, the charges used are huge as compared to the magnitude of electric charge. Hence, quantization of electric charge is of no use on macroscopic scale. Therefore, it is ignored and it is considered that electric charge is continuous.

question_text: When a glass rod is rubbed with a silk cloth, charges appear on both. A similar phenomenon is observed with many other pairs of bodies. Explain how this observation is consistent with the law of conservation of charge.
question_html: When a glass rod is rubbed with a silk cloth, charges appear on both. A similar phenomenon is observed with many other pairs of bodies. Explain how this observation is consistent with the law of conservation of charge.
solution_text: Total charge of an isolated system of objects is always conserved. As a consequence of conservation of charge, when two charged conductors of same size and same material carrying charges Q 1 and Q 2 respectively are brought in contact and separated, the charge on each conductor will be {tex}\frac{{{Q_1} + {Q_2}}}{2}{/tex} . This condition, however, does not hold true if the conductors are of different sizes or of different material. In that case the charges on the conductors will be {tex}Q_1'{/tex} and {tex}Q_2'{/tex} respectively, where {tex}{Q_1} + {Q_2} = Q_1' + Q_2'{/tex} . Example: When a glass rod is rubbed with silk cloth, glass rod becomes positively charged while silk cloth becomes negatively charged. The amount of positive charge on the glass rod is found to be exactly the same as negative charge on silk cloth. Thus, the system of glass rod and silk cloth, which was neutral before rubbing, still possesses no net charge after rubbing.
solution_html:
Total charge of an isolated system of objects is always conserved. As a consequence of conservation of charge, when two charged conductors of same size and same material carrying charges Q1 and Q2 respectively are brought in contact and separated, the charge on each conductor will be {tex}\frac{{{Q_1} + {Q_2}}}{2}{/tex}. This condition, however, does not hold true if the conductors are of different sizes or of different material. In that case the charges on the conductors will be {tex}Q_1'{/tex} and {tex}Q_2'{/tex} respectively, where {tex}{Q_1} + {Q_2} = Q_1' + Q_2'{/tex}.
Example: When a glass rod is rubbed with silk cloth, glass rod becomes positively charged while silk cloth becomes negatively charged. The amount of positive charge on the glass rod is found to be exactly the same as negative charge on silk cloth. Thus, the system of glass rod and silk cloth, which was neutral before rubbing, still possesses no net charge after rubbing.

question_text: Four point charges q A = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, q B = -5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, q C = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and q D = -5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C are located at the corners of a square ABCD of side 10 cm. What is the force on a charge of 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C placed at the centre of the square?
question_html: Four point charges qA = 2{tex}\mu{/tex}C, qB = -5{tex}\mu{/tex}C, qC = 2{tex}\mu{/tex}C and qD = -5{tex}\mu{/tex}C are located at the corners of a square ABCD of side 10 cm. What is the force on a charge of 1{tex}\mu{/tex}C placed at the centre of the square?
solution_text: Suppose a square ABCD with each side of 10 cm and centre O. At the centre, the charge of 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C is placed. Given: q A = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, q B = -5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, q C = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and q D = -5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C As q A = q C , the charge of 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C experiences equal and opposite forces F A and F C due to charges q A and q C . At the same time, the charge 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C experiences equal and opposite forces F B and F D due to equal charges q B and q D at B and D. Thus, the net force on charge of 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C due to the given charges is zero.
solution_html:
Suppose a square ABCD with each side of 10 cm and centre O. At the centre, the charge of 1{tex}\mu{/tex}C  is placed.

Given: qA = 2{tex}\mu{/tex}C, qB = -5{tex}\mu{/tex}C, qC = 2{tex}\mu{/tex}C and qD = -5{tex}\mu{/tex}C
As qA = qC, the charge of 1{tex}\mu{/tex}C experiences equal and opposite forces FA and FC due to charges qA and qC.
At the same time, the charge 1{tex}\mu{/tex}C experiences equal and opposite forces FB and FD due to equal charges qB and qD at B and D.
Thus, the net force on charge of 1{tex}\mu{/tex}C due to the given charges is zero.

question_text: An electrostatic field line is a continuous curve. That is, a field cannot have sudden breaks. Why not? Explain why two field lines never cross each other at any point?
question_html:
1. An electrostatic field line is a continuous curve. That is, a field cannot have sudden breaks. Why not?
  2. Explain why two field lines never cross each other at any point?
solution_text: An electrostatic field line is a continuous curve because a charge experiences a continuous force when traced in an electrostatic field. The field line cannot have sudden breaks because the charge moves continuously and does not jump from one point to the other. If two field lines cross each other at a point, then electric field intensity will show two directions at that point. This is not possible. Hence, two field lines never cross each other
solution_html:
1. An electrostatic field line is a continuous curve because a charge experiences a continuous force when traced in an electrostatic field. The field line cannot have sudden breaks because the charge moves continuously and does not jump from one point to the other.
  2. If two field lines cross each other at a point, then electric field intensity will show two directions at that point. This is not possible. Hence, two field lines never cross each other

question_text: Two-point charges q A = +3 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and q B = -3 {tex}\mu{/tex} C are located 20 cm apart in vacuum, What is the electric field at the midpoint O of the line AB joining the two charges?   2. If a negative test charge of magnitude 1{tex}\cdot{/tex}5{tex}\times{/tex}10-9 C is placed at this point, what is the force experienced by the test charge?
solution_text: Here, q A = 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} C = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C ; q B = -3 {tex}\mu{/tex} C = - 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C and r = 20 cm = 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 2 m Let O be the mid-point of the line AB as shown in Fig. Then, OA = OB = {tex}\frac{r}{2}=\frac{0 \cdot 2}{2}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1 m The electric field at point O due to q A E A = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{A}}{(O A)^{2}}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 {tex}\times \frac{3 \times 10^{-6}}{(0 \cdot 1)^{2}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 NC -1 (along OB) The electric field at point O due to q B , E B = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{A}}{(O B)^{2}}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 {tex}\times \frac{3 \times 10^{-6}}{(0-1)^{2}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 NC -1 (along OB) Therefore, net electric field at point O due to the charges q A and q B , E = E A + E B = 2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 + 2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 = 5 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 N C -1 (along OB) Force on a negative charge of magnitude 1 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 C placed at point O, F = qE = 1 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 = 8 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 N (along OA) The force on the negative charge acts in a direction opposite to that of the electric field.
solution_html:
Here, qA = 3{tex}\mu{/tex}C = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 C ;
qB = -3{tex}\mu{/tex}C = - 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 C and r = 20 cm = 0{tex}\cdot{/tex}2 m
Let O be the mid-point of the line AB as shown in Fig.

Then, OA = OB = {tex}\frac{r}{2}=\frac{0 \cdot 2}{2}{/tex} = 0{tex}\cdot{/tex}1 m

  1. The electric field at point O due to qA
    EA= {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{A}}{(O A)^{2}}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 {tex}\times \frac{3 \times 10^{-6}}{(0 \cdot 1)^{2}}{/tex}
    = 2{tex}\cdot{/tex}7 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 NC-1 (along OB)
    The electric field at point O due to qB,
    EB = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{A}}{(O B)^{2}}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 {tex}\times \frac{3 \times 10^{-6}}{(0-1)^{2}}{/tex}
    = 2{tex}\cdot{/tex}7 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 NC-1 (along OB)
    Therefore, net electric field at point O due to the charges qA and qB,
    E = EA + EB = 2{tex}\cdot{/tex}7 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 + 2{tex}\cdot{/tex}7 {tex}\times{/tex} 106
    = 5{tex}\cdot{/tex}4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 N C-1 (along OB)
  2. Force on a negative charge of magnitude 1{tex}\cdot{/tex}5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 C placed at point O,
    F = qE = 1{tex}\cdot{/tex}5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 {tex}\times{/tex} 5{tex}\cdot{/tex}4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106
    = 8{tex}\cdot{/tex}1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 N (along OA)
    The force on the negative charge acts in a direction opposite to that of the electric field.

question_text: A polythene piece rubbed with wool is found to have a negative charge of 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 C. Estimate the number of electrons transferred (from which to which)? Is there a transfer of mass from wool to polythene?
question_html:
A polythene piece rubbed with wool is found to have a negative charge of 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7C.

  1. Estimate the number of electrons transferred (from which to which)?
  2. Is there a transfer of mass from wool to polythene?
solution_text: When polythene is rubbed against wool, a number of electrons get transferred from wool to polythene. Hence, wool becomes positively charged and polythene becomes negatively charged. Amount of charge on the polythene piece, q = −3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −7 C Amount of charge on an electron, e = −1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −19 C Number of electrons transferred from wool to polythene = n n can be calculated using the relation, q = ne n = {tex}\frac{q}{e}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-3 \times 10^{-7}}{-1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 1.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 12 Therefore, the number of electrons transferred from wool to polythene is 1.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 12 . Yes. There is a transfer of mass occur. This is because an electron has mass, as we know m e = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −3 kg Total mass transferred from wool to polythene, m = me {tex}\times{/tex} n = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −31 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 12 = 1.706 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −18 kg Hence, a negligible amount of mass is transferred from wool to polythene.
solution_html:
1. When polythene is rubbed against wool, a number of electrons get transferred from wool to polythene. Hence, wool becomes positively charged and polythene becomes negatively charged.
    Amount of charge on the polythene piece, q = −3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−7 C
    Amount of charge on an electron, e = −1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−19 C
    Number of electrons transferred from wool to polythene = n
    n can be calculated using the relation, q = ne
    n = {tex}\frac{q}{e}{/tex} 
    = {tex}\frac{-3 \times 10^{-7}}{-1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} 
    = 1.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012 
    Therefore, the number of electrons transferred from wool to polythene is 1.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012.
  2. Yes. There is a transfer of mass occur. This is because an electron has mass, as we know
    me = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−3 kg Total mass transferred from wool to polythene,
    m = me {tex}\times{/tex} n
    = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−31 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.87{tex}\times{/tex} 1012 = 1.706 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−18 kg
    Hence, a negligible amount of mass is transferred from wool to polythene.

  2. What is the force of repulsion if each sphere is charged double the above amount, and the distance between them is halved?
solution_text: {tex}{q_1}={q_2} = 6.5 \times {10^{ - 7}}C{/tex} r = 50 cm = 0.50 m {tex}k = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} = 9 \times {10^9}N{m^2}{C^{ - 2}}{/tex} F = ? According to Coulomb's law, electrostatic force of attraction is given by; {tex}F = k\frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times 6.5 \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 6.5 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{{{(0.50)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}F = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}N{/tex} Now, if each sphere is charged double, and the distance between them is halved then the force of repulsion is: {tex}F = k \cdot \frac{{2{q_1}2{q_2}}}{{{{\left( {\frac{r}{2}} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}F = 16k \cdot \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = 16 \times 1.5 \times {10^{ - 2}} = 24 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} F = 0.24 N
solution_html:
1. {tex}{q_1}={q_2} = 6.5 \times {10^{ - 7}}C{/tex}
    r = 50 cm = 0.50 m
    {tex}k = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} = 9 \times {10^9}N{m^2}{C^{ - 2}}{/tex}
    F = ?
    According to Coulomb's law, electrostatic force of attraction is given by;
    {tex}F = k\frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times 6.5 \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 6.5 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{{{(0.50)}^2}}}{/tex}
    {tex}F = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}N{/tex}
  2. Now, if each sphere is charged double, and the distance between them is halved then the force of repulsion is:
    {tex}F = k \cdot \frac{{2{q_1}2{q_2}}}{{{{\left( {\frac{r}{2}} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
    {tex}F = 16k \cdot \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 16 \times 1.5 \times {10^{ - 2}} = 24 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex}
    F = 0.24 N

question_text: Figure shows tracks of three charged particles in a uniform electrostatic field. Give the signs of the three charges. Which particle has the highest charge to mass ratio?
question_html: Figure shows tracks of three charged particles in a uniform electrostatic field. Give the signs of the three charges. Which particle has the highest charge to mass ratio?
solution_text: Particles (1) and (2) are negatively charged and particle (3) is positively charged since the charged particles are deflected towards oppositely charged plates. Further, as the displacement y {tex}\propto\frac{e}{m}{/tex} therefore, particle (3) having a maximum value of y has the highest charge to mass ratio.

question_text: Consider a uniform electric field {tex}E = 3 \times {10^3}\hat i\;N/C{/tex} . What is the flux of this field through a square of 10 cm on a side whose plane is parallel to the yz plane? What is the flux through the same square if the normal to its plane makes a 60° angle with the x-axis?
question_html:
Consider a uniform electric field {tex}E = 3 \times {10^3}\hat i\;N/C{/tex}.

  1. What is the flux of this field through a square of 10 cm on a side whose plane is parallel to the yz plane?
  2. What is the flux through the same square if the normal to its plane makes a 60° angle with the x-axis?
solution_text: Given, {tex}\overrightarrow E = 3 \times {10^3}\hat i\;N{C^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta S{/tex} (Area of the square) {tex} = 10 \times 10 = 100c{m^2} = {10^{ - 2}}{m^{ 2}}{/tex} The area of a surface can be represented as a vector along normal to the surface. Since normal to the square is along x-axis, we have {tex}\Delta \overrightarrow S = {10^{ - 2}}\hat i\;{m^{ 2}}{/tex} Electric flux through the square is given by :- {tex}\phi = \overrightarrow E \cdot \overrightarrow {\Delta S} {/tex} = {tex}(3 \times {10^3}\hat i) \cdot ({10^{ - 2}}\hat i){/tex} {tex} = 30N{m^2}{C^{ - 1}}{/tex} Given, the angle between area vector and the electric field is 60°. Therefore, {tex}\phi = \overrightarrow E \cdot \Delta \overrightarrow S {/tex} {tex} = E \cdot \Delta S\;\cos 60^\circ {/tex} {tex} = 3 \times {10^3} \times {10^{ - 2}} \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex} = 15N{m^2}{C^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, {tex}\overrightarrow E = 3 \times {10^3}\hat i\;N{C^{ - 1}}{/tex}

  1. {tex}\Delta S{/tex} (Area of the square) {tex} = 10 \times 10 = 100c{m^2} = {10^{ - 2}}{m^{ 2}}{/tex}
    The area of a surface can be represented as a vector along normal to the surface. Since normal to the square is along x-axis, we have
    {tex}\Delta \overrightarrow S = {10^{ - 2}}\hat i\;{m^{ 2}}{/tex}

    Electric flux through the square is given by :-
    {tex}\phi = \overrightarrow E \cdot \overrightarrow {\Delta S} {/tex}
    = {tex}(3 \times {10^3}\hat i) \cdot ({10^{ - 2}}\hat i){/tex}
    {tex} = 30N{m^2}{C^{ - 1}}{/tex}
  2. Given, the angle between area vector and the electric field is 60°. Therefore,
    {tex}\phi = \overrightarrow E \cdot \Delta \overrightarrow S {/tex}
    {tex} = E \cdot \Delta S\;\cos 60^\circ {/tex}
    {tex} = 3 \times {10^3} \times {10^{ - 2}} \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    {tex} = 15N{m^2}{C^{ - 1}}{/tex}

question_text: What is the net flux of the uniform electric field of {tex}\vec E{/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\hat i{/tex} N/C through a cube of side 20 cm oriented so that its faces are parallel to the co-ordinate planes?
question_html: What is the net flux of the uniform electric field of {tex}\vec E{/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\hat i{/tex} N/C through a cube of side 20 cm oriented so that its faces are parallel to the co-ordinate planes?
solution_text: Here, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}}=3 \times 10^{3} \hat{i} \;\mathrm{NC}^{-1}{/tex} A = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 400 cm 2 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m 2 Through a face parallel to XY-plane : {tex}\vec{\mathrm{A}}_{1}=4 \times 10^{-2} \hat{k} \;m^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \phi_{1}=\vec{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{A}_{1}}=\left(3 \times 10^{3} \hat{i}\right) \cdot\left(4 \times 10^{-2} \hat{k}\right)=0{/tex} Through a face parallel to YZ-plane : {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}_{2}=4 \times 10^{-2} \hat{i} \;m^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \phi_{2}=\vec{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{A}_{2}}=\left(3 \times 10^{3} \hat{i}\right) \cdot\left(4 \times 10^{-2} \hat{i}\right){/tex} = 120 Nm 2 C -1 Through a face parallel to ZX-plane : {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}_{3}}=4 \times 10^{-2} \hat{j} \mathrm{m}^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \phi_{3}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}_{3}}=\left(3 \times 10^{3} \hat{i}\right) \cdot\left(4 \times 10^{-2} \hat{j}\right)=0{/tex} In each plane, there is a set of two faces of the cube. Faces of cube are parallel to the coordinate axes. Therefore, the number of field lines entering the cube is equal to the number of field lines coming out of the cube. Therefore, net flux through the cube, {tex}\phi=\left(\phi_{1}-\phi_{1}\right)+\left(\phi_{2}-\phi_{2}\right)+\left(\phi_{3}-\phi_{3}\right)=0{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}}=3 \times 10^{3} \hat{i} \;\mathrm{NC}^{-1}{/tex}
A = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 400 cm2 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 m2
Through a face parallel to XY-plane :
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{A}}_{1}=4 \times 10^{-2} \hat{k} \;m^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \phi_{1}=\vec{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{A}_{1}}=\left(3 \times 10^{3} \hat{i}\right) \cdot\left(4 \times 10^{-2} \hat{k}\right)=0{/tex}
Through a face parallel to YZ-plane :
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}_{2}=4 \times 10^{-2} \hat{i} \;m^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \phi_{2}=\vec{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{A}_{2}}=\left(3 \times 10^{3} \hat{i}\right) \cdot\left(4 \times 10^{-2} \hat{i}\right){/tex}
= 120 Nm2 C-1
Through a face parallel to ZX-plane :
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}_{3}}=4 \times 10^{-2} \hat{j} \mathrm{m}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \phi_{3}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}_{3}}=\left(3 \times 10^{3} \hat{i}\right) \cdot\left(4 \times 10^{-2} \hat{j}\right)=0{/tex}
In each plane, there is a set of two faces of the cube. Faces of cube  are parallel to the coordinate axes. Therefore, the number of field lines entering the cube is equal to the number of field lines coming out of the cube. Therefore, net flux through the cube,
{tex}\phi=\left(\phi_{1}-\phi_{1}\right)+\left(\phi_{2}-\phi_{2}\right)+\left(\phi_{3}-\phi_{3}\right)=0{/tex}

question_text: Careful measurement of the electric field at the surface of a black box indicates that the net outward flux through the surface of the box is 8.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 N m 2 C -1 . What is the net charge inside the box? If the net outward flux through the surface of the box were zero, could you conclude that there were no charges inside the box? Why or why not?
question_html:
Careful measurement of the electric field at the surface of a black box indicates that the net outward flux through the surface of the box is 8.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 N m2 C-1.

  1. What is the net charge inside the box?
  2. If the net outward flux through the surface of the box were zero, could you conclude that there were no charges inside the box? Why or why not?
solution_text: Here, {tex} \phi{/tex} = 8.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 3 Nm 2 C -1 Suppose that the net charge inside the box is q. Then, according to Gauss theorem, {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} or q = {tex}\varepsilon_{0} \phi{/tex} Now, {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = 8.854 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 -12 C 2 N -1 m -2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} q = 8.854 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 = 70.832 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 C If the net outward flux through the surface of the box is zero, it cannot be concluded that there is no charge inside the box. There may be equal amounts of positive and negative charges inside the box. Therefore, if the net outward flux is zero, we cannot conclude that the charge inside the box is zero. One can only say that the net charge inside the box is zero.
solution_html:
1. Here, {tex} \phi{/tex} = 8.0 {tex}\times {/tex}103 Nm2C-1
    Suppose that the net charge inside the box is q.
    Then, according to Gauss theorem,
    {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    or q = {tex}\varepsilon_{0} \phi{/tex}
    Now, {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = 8.854 {tex}\times {/tex}10-12 C2N-1m-2
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} q = 8.854 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 103
    = 70.832 {tex}\times{/tex}10-9 C
  2. If the net outward flux through the surface of the box is zero, it cannot be concluded that there is no charge inside the box. There may be equal amounts of positive and negative charges inside the box. Therefore, if the net outward flux is zero, we cannot conclude that the charge inside the box is zero. One can only say that the net charge inside the box is zero.

question_text: A point charge {tex} + 10\mu C{/tex} is a distance 5 cm directly above the center of a square of side 10 cm, as shown in figure. What is the magnitude of the electric flux through the square? (Hint: Think of the square as one face of a cube with edge 10 cm)
question_html: A point charge {tex} + 10\mu C{/tex} is a distance 5 cm directly above the center of a square of side 10 cm, as shown in figure. What is the magnitude of the electric flux through the square? (Hint: Think of the square as one face of a cube with edge 10 cm)
solution_text: Let us assume that the charge {tex}q = \pm 10\mu C = { \pm10^{ - 5}}C{/tex} is placed at a distance of 5 cm from the square ABCD of each side 10 cm. The square ABCD can be considered as one of the six faces of a cubic Gaussian surface of each side 10 cm. Now the total electric flux through the faces of the cube as per Gaussian theorem {tex}\phi = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} Therefore, the total electric flux through the square ABCD will be {tex}{\phi _E} = \frac{1}{6} \times \phi = \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{8.854 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 1.88 \times {10^5}{/tex} Nm 2 C -1 Therefore, electric flux through the square is 1.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 Nm 2 C -1 .
solution_html:
Let us assume that the charge {tex}q = \pm 10\mu C = { \pm10^{ - 5}}C{/tex}  is placed at a distance of 5 cm from the square ABCD of each side 10 cm. The square ABCD can be considered as one of the six faces of a cubic Gaussian surface of each side 10 cm.
Now the total electric flux through the faces of the cube as per Gaussian theorem
{tex}\phi = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
Therefore, the total electric flux through the square ABCD will be

{tex}{\phi _E} = \frac{1}{6} \times \phi = \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{8.854 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{/tex}
{tex} = 1.88 \times {10^5}{/tex} Nm2C-1
Therefore, electric flux through the square is 1.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 Nm2C-1.

question_text: A point charge causes an electric flux of {tex} - 1.0 \times {10^3}N{m^2}/C{/tex} to pass through a spherical Gaussian surface of 10.0 cm radius centered on the charge. If the radius of the Gaussian surface were doubled, how much flux would pass through the surface? What is the value of the point charge?
question_html:
A point charge causes an electric flux of {tex} - 1.0 \times {10^3}N{m^2}/C{/tex} to pass through a spherical Gaussian surface of 10.0 cm radius centered on the charge.

  1. If the radius of the Gaussian surface were doubled, how much flux would pass through the surface?
  2. What is the value of the point charge?
solution_text: Given, {tex}\phi = - 1.0 \times {10^3}N{m^2}/C{/tex} r 1 = 0.1m, r 2 = 0.2m Doubling the radius of Gaussian surface will not affect the electric flux since the charge enclosed is the same in the two cases. Thus, the flux will remain be the same i.e. {tex} - 1.0 \times {10^3}N{m^2}/C{/tex} {tex}\phi = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore q = \phi \cdot {\varepsilon _0}{/tex} or {tex}q = - 1.0 \times {10^3} \times 8.854 \times {10^{ - 12}}{/tex} {tex} = - 8.854 \times {10^{ - 9}}C{/tex} = -8.854 nC Therefore, the value of the point charge is-8.854 nC.
solution_html:
Given, {tex}\phi = - 1.0 \times {10^3}N{m^2}/C{/tex}
r1 = 0.1m, r2 = 0.2m

  1. Doubling the radius of Gaussian surface will not affect the electric flux since the charge enclosed is the same in the two cases.
    Thus, the flux will remain be the same i.e. {tex} - 1.0 \times {10^3}N{m^2}/C{/tex}
  2. {tex}\phi = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore q = \phi \cdot {\varepsilon _0}{/tex}
    or {tex}q = - 1.0 \times {10^3} \times 8.854 \times {10^{ - 12}}{/tex}
    {tex} = - 8.854 \times {10^{ - 9}}C{/tex}
    = -8.854 nC 
    Therefore, the value of the point charge is-8.854 nC.

question_text: A conducting sphere of radius 10 cm has an unknown charge. If the electric field, 20 cm from the centre of the sphere is {tex}1.5 \times {10^3}N/C{/tex} and points radially inward, what is the net charge on the sphere?
question_html: A conducting sphere of radius 10 cm has an unknown charge. If the electric field, 20 cm from the centre of the sphere is {tex}1.5 \times {10^3}N/C{/tex}  and points radially inward, what is the net charge on the sphere?
solution_text: Given, r = 10cm = 0.1m {tex}E = 1.5 \times {10^3}N/C{/tex} at d = 0.2 m As {tex}E = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{q}{{{d^2}}}{/tex} then, {tex}q = E \cdot 4\pi {\varepsilon _0} \cdot {d^2}{/tex} or, {tex}q = 1.5 \times {10^3} \times 4\pi \times \left( {\frac{1}{{4\pi \times 9 \times {{10}^9}}}} \right) \times {(0.2)^2}{/tex} {tex} = 6.67 \times {10^{ - 9}}C{/tex} Here, q is negative since electric field is directed inward. Thus, {tex}q = -6.67 \times {10^{ - 9}}C{/tex} = -6.67nC
solution_html:
Given, r = 10cm = 0.1m
{tex}E = 1.5 \times {10^3}N/C{/tex} at d = 0.2 m
As {tex}E = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{q}{{{d^2}}}{/tex}
then, {tex}q = E \cdot 4\pi {\varepsilon _0} \cdot {d^2}{/tex}
or, {tex}q = 1.5 \times {10^3} \times 4\pi \times \left( {\frac{1}{{4\pi \times 9 \times {{10}^9}}}} \right) \times {(0.2)^2}{/tex}
{tex} = 6.67 \times {10^{ - 9}}C{/tex}
Here, q is negative since electric field is directed inward.
Thus, {tex}q = -6.67 \times {10^{ - 9}}C{/tex} = -6.67nC

question_text: A uniformly charged conducting sphere of 2.4 m diameter has a surface charge density of 80.0 {tex}\mu{/tex} Cm -2 . Find the charge on the sphere. What is the total electric flux leaving the surface of the sphere?
question_html:
A uniformly charged conducting sphere of 2.4 m diameter has a surface charge density of 80.0 {tex}\mu{/tex}Cm-2.

  1. Find the charge on the sphere.
  2. What is the total electric flux leaving the surface of the sphere?
solution_text: Radius of sphere, {tex}R = \frac { d } { 2 } = \frac { 2.4 } { 2 } = 1.2 \mathrm { m }{/tex} Surface charge density, {tex}\sigma = 80 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { Cm } ^ { - 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \sigma = \frac { q } { 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } } {/tex} or {tex}q = 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma{/tex} Charge on the sphere is given by (q) {tex}q = 4 \times 3.14 \times (1.2 ) ^ { 2 } \times 80 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { }{/tex} {tex}q = 1.447 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} Gauss’s law states that the net flux of an electric field in a closed surface is directly proportional to the enclosed electric charge. Electric flux is given by, {tex}\phi = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} {tex}\phi = \frac { 1.447 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } { 8.85 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } } {/tex} = {tex} 1.63 \times 10 ^ { 8 }{/tex} Nm 2 C -1
solution_html:
1. Radius of sphere, {tex}R = \frac { d } { 2 } = \frac { 2.4 } { 2 } = 1.2 \mathrm { m }{/tex}
    Surface charge density, {tex}\sigma = 80 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { Cm } ^ { - 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \sigma = \frac { q } { 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } } {/tex} or {tex}q = 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma{/tex}
    Charge on the sphere is given by (q)
    {tex}q = 4 \times 3.14 \times (1.2 ) ^ { 2 } \times 80 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { }{/tex}
    {tex}q = 1.447 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { C }{/tex}
  2. Gauss’s law states that the net flux of an electric field in a closed surface is directly proportional to the enclosed electric charge.
    Electric flux is given by, {tex}\phi = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\phi = \frac { 1.447 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } { 8.85 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } } {/tex} = {tex} 1.63 \times 10 ^ { 8 }{/tex} Nm2C-1

question_text: An infinite line charge produces a field of 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 NC -1 at distance of 2 cm. Calculate the linear charge density.
question_html: An infinite line charge produces a field of 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 NC-1 at distance of 2 cm. Calculate the linear charge density.
solution_text: Step 1: Electric field due ti infinite line charge Electric field due to infinite line charge is given by: {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 \mathrm{~d}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda=2 \pi \epsilon_0 \mathrm{E} \mathrm{d}{/tex} ...(1) Where {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the linear charge density and d is the perpendicular distance of point from the infinite line charge. Step: 2 Substituting the values {tex}\because \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}=9 \times 10^9 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \pi \epsilon_0=\frac{1}{18 \times 10^9 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-2}}{/tex} From Eq (1): {tex}\lambda=\frac{1 \times 9 \times 10^4 \mathrm{NC}^{-1} \times 0.02 \mathrm{~m}}{18 \times 10^9 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-2}}=10^{-7} \mathrm{Cm}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
Step 1: Electric field due ti infinite line charge
Electric field due to infinite line charge is given by:
{tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 \mathrm{~d}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda=2 \pi \epsilon_0 \mathrm{E} \mathrm{d}{/tex} ...(1)
Where {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the linear charge density and d is the perpendicular distance of point from the infinite line charge.
Step: 2 Substituting the values
{tex}\because \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}=9 \times 10^9 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \pi \epsilon_0=\frac{1}{18 \times 10^9 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-2}}{/tex}
From Eq (1): {tex}\lambda=\frac{1 \times 9 \times 10^4 \mathrm{NC}^{-1} \times 0.02 \mathrm{~m}}{18 \times 10^9 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-2}}=10^{-7} \mathrm{Cm}^{-1}{/tex}

question_text: Two large, thin metal plates are parallel and close to each other. On their inner faces, the plates have surface charge densities of opposite signs and of magnitude 17.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -22 C/m 2 . What is E: in the outer region of the first plate, in the outer region of the second plate, and between the plates?
question_html:
Two large, thin metal plates are parallel and close to each other. On their inner faces, the plates have surface charge densities of opposite signs and of magnitude 17.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22 C/m2. What is E:

  1. in the outer region of the first plate,
  2. in the outer region of the second plate, and
  3. between the plates?
solution_text: The situation is represented in the following figure. A and B are two parallel plates close to each other. subject: Physics
question_text: It is now believed that protons and neutrons (which constitute nuclei of ordinary matter) are themselves built out of more elementary units called quarks. A proton and a neutron consists of three quarks each. Two types of quarks, the so called 'up' quark (denoted by u) of charge + (2/3) e, and the 'down' quark (denoted by d) of charge , together with electrons build up ordinary matter. (Quarks of other types have also been found which give rise to different unusual varieties of matter). Suggest a possible quark composition of a proton and a neutron.
question_html: It is now believed that protons and neutrons (which constitute nuclei of ordinary matter) are themselves built out of more elementary units called quarks. A proton and a neutron consists of three quarks each. Two types of quarks, the so called 'up' quark (denoted by u) of charge + (2/3) e, and the 'down' quark (denoted by d) of charge , together with electrons build up ordinary matter. (Quarks of other types have also been found which give rise to different unusual varieties of matter). Suggest a possible quark composition of a proton and a neutron.
solution_text: Charge on 'up' quark {tex}(u) = + \frac{2}{3}e{/tex} Charge on 'down' quark {tex}(d) = - \frac{1}{3}e{/tex} Charge on a proton = e Charge on a neutron = 0 Let a proton contains x 'up' quarks and (3 - x) 'down' quarks. Then total charge on a proton is, ux + d(3 - x) = e or, {tex} + \frac{2}{3}ex - \frac{1}{3}e(3 - x) = e{/tex} or, {tex} + \frac{2}{3}x - 1 + \frac{x}{3} = 1{/tex} or, x = 2 and 3 - x = 3 - 2 = 1 i.e. proton contains 2 'up' quarks and 1 'down' quark. Its quarks composition should be uud. Let a neutron contains y 'up' quarks and (3 - y) 'down' quarks. Then total charge on a neutron is: ny + d(3 - y) = 0 or, {tex} + \frac{2}{3}ey - \frac{1}{3}e(3 - y) = 0{/tex} or, {tex} + \frac{2}{3}y - 1 + \frac{y}{3} = 1{/tex} or, y = 1 and 3 - y = 3 - 1 = 2 i.e. neutrons contain 1 'up' quark and 2 'down' quarks. Its quark composition should be udd.
solution_html:
Charge on 'up' quark {tex}(u) = + \frac{2}{3}e{/tex}
Charge on 'down' quark {tex}(d) = - \frac{1}{3}e{/tex}
Charge on a proton = e
Charge on a neutron = 0
Let a proton contains x 'up' quarks and (3 - x) 'down' quarks. Then total charge on a proton is,
ux + d(3 - x) = e
or, {tex} + \frac{2}{3}ex - \frac{1}{3}e(3 - x) = e{/tex}
or, {tex} + \frac{2}{3}x - 1 + \frac{x}{3} = 1{/tex}
or, x = 2
and 3 - x = 3 - 2 = 1
i.e. proton contains 2 'up' quarks and 1 'down' quark. Its quarks composition should be uud.
Let a neutron contains y 'up' quarks and (3 - y) 'down' quarks.
Then total charge on a neutron is:
ny + d(3 - y) = 0
or, {tex} + \frac{2}{3}ey - \frac{1}{3}e(3 - y) = 0{/tex}
or, {tex} + \frac{2}{3}y - 1 + \frac{y}{3} = 1{/tex}
or, y = 1
and 3 - y = 3 - 1 = 2
i.e. neutrons contain 1 'up' quark and 2 'down' quarks. Its quark composition should be udd.
